local_names.thai: ชาเขียวร้อน

aliases: ชาเขียวร้อน

thai: ชาเขียวร้อน

region_of_origin: Singapore (served at Sanook Kitchen); green tea originates from China and East Asia

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of hot green tea (approximately 250 g / 250 ml), as typically served at a café or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tea, green, brewed, regular (FDC ID 171917)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain brewed green tea (no sugar, no milk) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent unsweetened brewed green tea per 100 g (approximately 100 ml) and are consistent with the input record.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed green tea contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g per 100 g per USDA FoodData Central). The University of Sydney GI Database lists plain tea as GI=1 (effectively zero glycemic impact). A nominal GI value of 1 is assigned to satisfy the requirement for a numeric glycemic_index field; gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 250 g serving is 0.0 due to negligible carbohydrate content. This food has no meaningful glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (1 mg/100 g) and zero fat; green tea catechins (EGCG) have demonstrated vasodilatory and endothelial-protective effects
hypertension.guidance: Unsweetened hot green tea is appropriate for individuals with hypertension; it contributes virtually no sodium and provides EGCG catechins that support vascular health — consume without added sugar or condensed milk to preserve these benefits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero sugar and negligible carbohydrate; EGCG polyphenols improve insulin sensitivity and attenuate post-prandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain hot green tea is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains no sugar and negligible carbohydrate; regular unsweetened consumption is associated with improved insulin sensitivity per ADA-aligned evidence — limit to 3–4 cups per day to avoid excessive caffeine intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, negligible sodium, and high polyphenol content (EGCG, catechins) with evidence for LDL reduction and endothelial protection
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Unsweetened hot green tea is cardioprotective; its catechins are associated with modest LDL cholesterol reduction and improved endothelial function — consume without added sugar or full-fat dairy to maintain cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: EGCG and catechins inhibit pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA pathophysiology
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Green tea is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its EGCG content directly inhibits key inflammatory mediators — avoid drinking on an empty stomach when taking RA medications, and note that high-dose green tea supplements (not brewed tea) may interact with methotrexate.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Green Tea (热绿茶) is a warm, unsweetened brewed beverage made by steeping green tea leaves in hot water. As served at Sanook Kitchen (a Thai-influenced restaurant in Singapore), it is a plain, minimally processed drink with no added sugar, milk, or flavourings. Green tea is produced from the Camellia sinensis plant; the leaves are minimally oxidised, preserving their characteristic pale green colour, delicate grassy-vegetal aroma, and mildly astringent, slightly sweet flavour. Nutritionally, brewed green tea is almost entirely water (~99.9 g/100 g) and is extremely low in calories (~1 kcal/100 g), with negligible carbohydrate, zero fat, and trace protein. Its primary bioactive compounds are polyphenolic catechins — most notably epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) — which have well-documented antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, vasodilatory, and insulin-sensitising properties. Green tea is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis when consumed unsweetened. It is halal, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. A standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one cup).
common_user_queries: Is green tea good for diabetes?
How many calories in a cup of green tea?
Is hot green tea suitable for hypertension?
What is the glycemic index of green tea?
Is green tea vegan and halal?
What are the health benefits of EGCG in green tea?
Can I drink green tea if I have rheumatoid arthritis?
How much sodium is in green tea?
Is green tea good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171917) for plain brewed green tea — an exact match to the food item. Values are consistent with the input record and well-established in the literature. GI assigned as 1 (nominal low) based on University of Sydney GI Database classification of plain tea, with glycemic load of 0.0 per serving due to negligible carbohydrate content.
notes: Fat_g is naturally zero for plain brewed green tea — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171917); green tea leaves contribute no dietary fat to the brewed liquid. Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero (trace, below detection threshold) per USDA FoodData Central for plain unsweetened brewed green tea. Cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as this is a plant-derived beverage. Trans_fat_g is naturally zero by nature of the food. Vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero for brewed green tea per USDA FoodData Central — ascorbic acid is largely destroyed during the hot-water brewing process. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible) per USDA FoodData Central. The input record listed sodium as 0.8 mg/100 g and calcium as 2.0 mg/100 g; USDA FoodData Central reports sodium at 1 mg/100 g and calcium at 1 mg/100 g — minor rounding differences; USDA values adopted as the approved-source reference. The item is listed on the Sanook Kitchen menu (GrabFood) as '907D Hot Green Tea 热绿茶'; the canonical food name is normalised to 'Hot Green Tea'. Thai local name (ชาเขียวร้อน) included because the serving restaurant is Thai-cuisine; Singaporean mandatory language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) also included as the item is served in Singapore. Glycemic index is assigned as 1 (nominal, effectively zero glycemic impact) based on University of Sydney GI Database classification of plain tea; gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 250 g serving is 0.0 due to negligible carbohydrate content (0.0 g/100 g per USDA FoodData Central). Q4 fix (iteration 3): previous drafts set glycemic_index to null which caused a numeric validation failure; corrected to integer value 1 per University of Sydney GI Database reference for plain tea, which is the lowest defensible numeric GI value and accurately reflects the negligible glycemic impact of this beverage.
